context_en: Miguel de Cervantes published the 1st part in 1605 and the 2nd in 1615. Considered the first modern novel.

question_en: In which century did Shakespeare live?
context_en: William Shakespeare (1564-1616). He wrote 39 plays and 154 sonnets.

question_en: Who wrote 'One Hundred Years of Solitude'?
context_en: Gabriel García Márquez published the novel in 1967. Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982. A pinnacle work of magical realism.

question_en: Which English author created Sherlock Holmes?
context_en: Arthur Conan Doyle created the detective Sherlock Holmes in 'A Study in Scarlet' (1887). He wrote 4 novels and 56 stories about him.
Jane Austen published 'Pride and Prejudice' in 1813. It is one of the most-read novels in the English language.
'Hamlet, Prince of Denmark' (1600-1601) is Shakespeare's longest tragedy. 'To be or not to be' is its most famous soliloquy.

question_en: Which country was Franz Kafka from?
context_en: Kafka (1883-1924) was born in Prague, then part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, today the Czech Republic. He wrote in German.
Franz Kafka published 'Die Verwandlung' (The Metamorphosis) in 1915. Gregor Samsa wakes up transformed into an insect.
Jorge Luis Borges published 'Ficciones' in 1944, one of the most influential short-story collections of the 20th century.
Fyodor Dostoevsky published 'Crime and Punishment' in 1866. It follows Raskolnikov after murdering a pawnbroker.
Gustave Flaubert published 'Madame Bovary' in 1856. It brought him a trial for immorality, of which he was acquitted.
Antoine de Saint-Exupéry published 'Le Petit Prince' in 1943. It is one of the most translated books in the world (more than 300 languages).
George Orwell published '1984' in 1949. A totalitarian dystopia that coined terms like 'Big Brother' and 'doublethink'.
Aldous Huxley published 'Brave New World' in 1932, a dystopia centered on control through pleasure and genetic engineering.
Victor Hugo published 'Les Misérables' in 1862. It follows the redemption of Jean Valjean in post-Napoleonic France.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Name of the Rose'?
context_en: Umberto Eco published 'Il nome della rosa' in 1980. A medieval mystery in a Benedictine monastery.

question_en: Which book begins with 'Call me Ishmael'?
context_en: 'Moby Dick' by Herman Melville (1851) begins with 'Call me Ishmael', one of the most famous opening lines in literature.
Bram Stoker published 'Dracula' in 1897, in epistolary form. It laid the foundations of the modern vampire novel.
Mary Shelley published 'Frankenstein, or the Modern Prometheus' in 1818, at just 20 years old. Considered a precursor of science fiction.

question_en: Which author wrote 'Don Quixote' 400 years before the 4th centenary celebrated in 2005?
context_en: Cervantes published the 1st part in 1605 (400 years before 2005). The 2nd came out in 1615.
F. Scott Fitzgerald published 'The Great Gatsby' in 1925. Considered one of the great American novels, a portrait of the Jazz Age.

question_en: Which prize did Hemingway and Steinbeck win?
context_en: Ernest Hemingway won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954 and John Steinbeck in 1962.
Albert Camus published 'L'Étranger' in 1942, considered a key work of existentialism (although Camus preferred 'absurdism').

question_en: Who wrote 'The Picture of Dorian Gray'?
context_en: Oscar Wilde published 'The Picture of Dorian Gray' in 1890. It is his only novel; the rest of his work is theater and poetry.

question_en: Which author created the detective Hercule Poirot?
context_en: Agatha Christie created Poirot in 'The Mysterious Affair at Styles' (1920). He appears in 33 novels and 50 stories. She is the best-selling fiction author.
James Joyce published 'Ulysses' in 1922. It narrates one day (16 June 1904) in Dublin. It innovated with the 'stream of consciousness'.

question_en: Which Brazilian author wrote 'Captains of the Sands'?
context_en: Jorge Amado published 'Capitães da Areia' in 1937. It portrays street children in Salvador de Bahia.
Julio Cortázar published 'Hopscotch' in 1963. An experimental novel that can be read in different orders.
Emily Brontë published 'Wuthering Heights' in 1847 under a male pseudonym. It is her only novel.

question_en: Which author wrote the 'La Comédie humaine' saga?
context_en: Honoré de Balzac wrote 'La Comédie humaine' (1830-1850), a set of ~90 interconnected works portraying French society.

question_en: Which Portuguese author won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1998?
context_en: José Saramago won the Nobel in 1998. His work includes 'Blindness' (1995) and 'The Year of the Death of Ricardo Reis'.
Leo Tolstoy published 'Anna Karenina' between 1875 and 1877.
Daniel Defoe published 'Robinson Crusoe' in 1719. Considered one of the first novels in English.
J.K. Rowling published 'Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone' in 1997. The saga sold more than 600 million copies.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Lord of the Rings'?
context_en: J.R.R. Tolkien published 'The Lord of the Rings' between 1954 and 1955. It laid the foundations of modern epic fantasy.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Pillars of the Earth'?
context_en: Ken Follett published 'The Pillars of the Earth' in 1989. A historical saga about the construction of a cathedral in medieval England.

question_en: Which Chilean author wrote 'The House of the Spirits'?
context_en: Isabel Allende published 'The House of the Spirits' in 1982. A family saga with magical realism, in an unnamed Chile.
Dante Alighieri wrote 'La Divina Commedia' around 1320. A pinnacle work of medieval Italian literature.
Homer (8th century BC) composed 'The Iliad' and 'The Odyssey'. His historical existence is debated (the 'Homeric question').

question_en: What is the first book of the Bible?
context_en: Genesis is the first book of the Old Testament. It narrates the creation of the world and the origins of humanity.
Jean-Paul Sartre published 'La Nausée' in 1938. A founding work of French existentialism.

question_en: Which Russian author wrote 'War and Peace'?
context_en: Leo Tolstoy published 'War and Peace' between 1865 and 1869. More than 1,200 pages and 580 characters during the Napoleonic Wars.
Dan Brown published 'The Da Vinci Code' in 2003. It sold more than 80 million copies.

question_en: Which Japanese author won the Nobel in 1968?
context_en: Yasunari Kawabata was the first Japanese author to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, in 1968. Author of 'Snow Country' and 'Thousand Cranes'.
Vladimir Nabokov published 'Lolita' in 1955. Controversial for its subject; acclaimed for its prose in English (his second language).
Paulo Coelho published 'O Alquimista' in 1988. It is the most translated Brazilian book (more than 80 languages).

question_en: Who wrote the novel 'Love in the Time of Cholera'?
context_en: García Márquez published 'Love in the Time of Cholera' in 1985, a novel inspired by his parents' love.

question_en: Which author published 'The Feast of the Goat'?
context_en: Mario Vargas Llosa published 'The Feast of the Goat' in 2000. It deals with the Trujillo dictatorship in the Dominican Republic.

question_en: In which century was Federico García Lorca born?
context_en: Lorca was born in 1898 (19th century) and was murdered in 1936 (20th century). A prominent member of the Generation of '27.
Juan Rulfo published 'Pedro Páramo' in 1955. A fundamental work of Latin American magical realism, a forerunner of García Márquez.
William Shakespeare wrote 'Romeo and Juliet' around 1595, one of the most famous tragedies in world literature.
William Shakespeare wrote 'Macbeth' (~1606), a tragedy set in medieval Scotland.

question_en: How many plays did Shakespeare write approximately?
context_en: Shakespeare is credited with 37-39 plays and 154 sonnets.
William Shakespeare wrote 'Othello' around 1603, a tragedy about jealousy.
Shakespeare wrote 'King Lear' (~1606), considered one of his deepest tragedies.
Pedro Calderón de la Barca wrote 'Life Is a Dream' (1635), a pinnacle work of Spanish Baroque theater.
Lope de Vega wrote 'Fuenteovejuna' (~1612-1614), based on real events from the 15th century.

question_en: How many plays did Lope de Vega write (approx.)?
context_en: Lope de Vega is credited with more than 1,800 plays, although only about 400-500 have survived.
Tirso de Molina wrote 'The Trickster of Seville' (~1630), in which the character of Don Juan appears for the first time.

question_en: How many parts does Don Quixote have?
context_en: Don Quixote was published in two parts: 1605 and 1615, both written by Miguel de Cervantes.

question_en: In what year was the first part of Don Quixote published?
context_en: The first part of Don Quixote was published in 1605. The second in 1615.

question_en: What is the name of Don Quixote's horse?
context_en: Don Quixote's horse is called Rocinante. Sancho rides Rucio (a donkey).

question_en: What is the name of Don Quixote's squire?
context_en: Sancho Panza is Don Quixote's loyal squire throughout the entire novel.
'Lazarillo de Tormes' (1554) is anonymous. It is the first example of the Spanish picaresque novel.

question_en: Who wrote 'El sí de las niñas'?
context_en: Leandro Fernández de Moratín wrote 'El sí de las niñas' (1806), a pinnacle work of Spanish Enlightenment theater.
Federico García Lorca wrote 'Blood Wedding' (1933), part of his rural trilogy.

question_en: Who wrote 'The House of Bernarda Alba'?
context_en: García Lorca finished 'The House of Bernarda Alba' in 1936, two months before being murdered.

question_en: In what year was Federico García Lorca murdered?
context_en: García Lorca was murdered by Francoist troops on 18 or 19 August 1936 near Granada.
Juan Ramón Jiménez wrote 'Platero and I' (1914), lyrical prose about his donkey Platero. Nobel Prize in Literature 1956.
Antonio Machado published 'Campos de Castilla' in 1912. He is linked to the Generation of '98.
Miguel de Unamuno published 'Niebla' in 1914, a novel in which the protagonist speaks with his author.
Leopoldo Alas 'Clarín' published 'La Regenta' (1884-1885), a pinnacle work of Spanish realism.
Benito Pérez Galdós published 'Fortunata y Jacinta' (1887), his greatest novel.
Benito Pérez Galdós wrote 46 'National Episodes' about the history of Spain in the 19th century.
Emilia Pardo Bazán published 'The House of Ulloa' in 1886, a naturalist novel set in Galicia.
Camilo José Cela published 'The Hive' in 1951. Nobel Prize in Literature 1989.

question_en: In what year did Cela win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
context_en: Camilo José Cela won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1989.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Family of Pascual Duarte'?
context_en: Camilo José Cela published 'The Family of Pascual Duarte' in 1942. It started the 'tremendismo' movement.
Miguel Delibes published 'The Holy Innocents' in 1981. It was adapted into a film by Mario Camus in 1984.
Pío Baroja published 'The Tree of Knowledge' in 1911, part of the trilogy 'The Race'.

question_en: Who is the author of 'Bohemian Lights' (Luces de bohemia)?
context_en: Ramón María del Valle-Inclán wrote 'Bohemian Lights' (1920), a pioneer of the 'esperpento'.
Luis Martín-Santos published 'Time of Silence' in 1962, a key novel in the renewal of Spanish realism.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Shadow of the Wind'?
context_en: Carlos Ruiz Zafón published 'The Shadow of the Wind' in 2001. The first novel of 'The Cemetery of Forgotten Books'.
Arturo Pérez-Reverte published 'The Club Dumas' in 1993. It inspired Polanski's film 'The Ninth Gate'.
Arturo Pérez-Reverte created Captain Alatriste in 1996, in a saga of historical novels.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Truth About the Savolta Case'?
context_en: Eduardo Mendoza published 'The Truth About the Savolta Case' in 1975, considered the start of the new Spanish narrative.
Javier Cercas published 'Soldiers of Salamis' in 2001, about an episode of the Civil War.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Time in Between' (El tiempo entre costuras)?
context_en: María Dueñas published 'The Time in Between' in 2009. Adapted into a TV series in 2013.
(A tricky question.) Sara Mesa published 'Bestiario íntimo', but 'Bestias y rituales' is attributed to her. Verify the exact title.
Mario Vargas Llosa (1936-) is a Peruvian writer. He won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2010.

question_en: Which author wrote 'The Time of the Hero'?
context_en: Mario Vargas Llosa published 'The Time of the Hero' in 1963, his first novel. It won the Biblioteca Breve Prize.
Mario Vargas Llosa published 'Conversation in the Cathedral' in 1969. One of his most complex works.

question_en: Who wrote 'Captain Pantoja and the Special Service'?
context_en: Mario Vargas Llosa published 'Captain Pantoja and the Special Service' in 1973, a political and military satire.
Gabriel García Márquez (1927-2014) was a Colombian writer. He won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982.

question_en: In what year did García Márquez receive the Nobel?
context_en: García Márquez received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982.

question_en: Which author wrote 'Chronicle of a Death Foretold'?
context_en: Gabriel García Márquez published 'Chronicle of a Death Foretold' in 1981.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Autumn of the Patriarch'?
context_en: Gabriel García Márquez published 'The Autumn of the Patriarch' in 1975, about a Caribbean dictator.

question_en: Who is the creator of Macondo?
context_en: Gabriel García Márquez created the fictional town of Macondo in 'One Hundred Years of Solitude' (1967).
Juan Rulfo published 'Pedro Páramo' in 1955. One of the key works of 20th-century Mexican literature.

question_en: Who wrote 'Where the Air Is Clear'?
context_en: Carlos Fuentes published 'Where the Air Is Clear' in 1958, his first novel. About Mexico City.
Carlos Fuentes published the novella 'Aura' in 1962, narrated in the second person.
Octavio Paz (1914-1998) was a Mexican poet and essayist. He won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1990.

question_en: Which essay by Octavio Paz reflects on Mexican identity?
context_en: 'The Labyrinth of Solitude' (1950) is Octavio Paz's emblematic work about the Mexican being.
Jorge Luis Borges published 'Ficciones' (1944) and 'The Aleph' (1949). A master of the short story.
Julio Cortázar published 'Hopscotch' in 1963, an experimental novel that can be read in different orders.
Ernesto Sábato published 'On Heroes and Tombs' in 1961, one of the great Argentine novels of the 20th century.
Ernesto Sábato published 'The Tunnel' in 1948. A short existentialist novel.
Pablo Neruda (1904-1973) was a Chilean poet. He won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1971.

question_en: Which book by Neruda contains 'Tonight I can write the saddest lines'?
context_en: Poem XX belongs to 'Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair' (1924) by Pablo Neruda.

options_en: A Chilean poet, the first Latin American Nobel
context_en: Gabriela Mistral (1889-1957) was the first Latin American author to win the Nobel Prize in Literature (1945).
Roberto Bolaño published 'The Savage Detectives' in 1998. It won the Rómulo Gallegos and the Herralde prizes.
Roberto Bolaño published '2666' in 2004, posthumously. A monumental novel about the Ciudad Juárez murders.
Marguerite Duras published 'The Lover' in 1984. It won the Prix Goncourt.
Marguerite Yourcenar published 'Memoirs of Hadrian' in 1951. The first woman in the French Academy (1980).

question_en: Who wrote 'The Red and the Black'?
context_en: Stendhal (Henri Beyle) published 'The Red and the Black' in 1830, a key novel of early French realism.
Honoré de Balzac published 'Eugénie Grandet' in 1833. Part of 'La Comédie humaine'.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Count of Monte Cristo'?
context_en: Alexandre Dumas (father) published 'The Count of Monte Cristo' between 1844 and 1846.
Alexandre Dumas (father) published 'The Three Musketeers' in 1844: D'Artagnan, Athos, Porthos and Aramis.
Victor Hugo published 'The Hunchback of Notre-Dame' in 1831. It stars Quasimodo and Esmeralda.
Daniel Defoe published 'Robinson Crusoe' in 1719. Considered one of the first modern English novels.
Jonathan Swift published 'Gulliver's Travels' in 1726, a political and philosophical satire.
Charles Dickens published 'Oliver Twist' between 1837 and 1839, about an orphan in Victorian London.
Charles Dickens published 'A Christmas Carol' in 1843, with Ebenezer Scrooge as the protagonist.
Charles Dickens published 'David Copperfield' in 1849-1850, his favorite and semi-autobiographical novel.
Emily Brontë published 'Wuthering Heights' in 1847, her only novel.
Charlotte Brontë published 'Jane Eyre' in 1847, one of the great English novels of the 19th century.
Jane Austen published 'Sense and Sensibility' in 1811, her first published novel.
Jane Austen published 'Emma' in 1815. One of her six major novels.
Jane Austen (1775-1817) wrote 6 novels that portray the rural English society of her time.
Herman Melville published 'Moby Dick' in 1851. It tells of Captain Ahab's pursuit of the white whale.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn'?
context_en: Mark Twain (Samuel Clemens) published 'The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn' in 1884.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Adventures of Tom Sawyer'?
context_en: Mark Twain published 'The Adventures of Tom Sawyer' in 1876.
Nathaniel Hawthorne published 'The Scarlet Letter' in 1850.

question_en: Who is the author of 'The Black Cat' and 'The Raven'?
context_en: Edgar Allan Poe (1809-1849) wrote those works. A pioneer of the modern short story and horror literature.
Walt Whitman published 'Leaves of Grass' in 1855, a founding work of American poetry.

question_en: Who wrote 'For Whom the Bell Tolls'?
context_en: Ernest Hemingway published 'For Whom the Bell Tolls' in 1940. Set in the Spanish Civil War.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Old Man and the Sea'?
context_en: Ernest Hemingway published 'The Old Man and the Sea' in 1952. It earned him the Pulitzer (1953) and the Nobel (1954).

question_en: Who wrote 'The Sound and the Fury'?
context_en: William Faulkner published 'The Sound and the Fury' in 1929. Nobel Prize in Literature 1949.
John Steinbeck published 'The Grapes of Wrath' in 1939. Pulitzer 1940 and Nobel 1962.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Catcher in the Rye'?
context_en: J.D. Salinger published 'The Catcher in the Rye' in 1951. It stars Holden Caulfield.
Truman Capote published 'In Cold Blood' in 1966, a pioneer of the 'non-fiction novel'.
Jack Kerouac published 'On the Road' in 1957. A manifesto of the Beat Generation.
Toni Morrison published 'Beloved' in 1987. Pulitzer 1988. Nobel Prize in Literature 1993.
Alice Walker published 'The Color Purple' in 1982. Pulitzer 1983.
Franz Kafka wrote 'The Trial', published posthumously in 1925 by his friend Max Brod.
Franz Kafka wrote 'The Castle', published posthumously in 1926. Unfinished.
Thomas Mann published 'The Magic Mountain' in 1924. Nobel Prize in Literature 1929.
Hermann Hesse published 'Demian' in 1919. Nobel Prize in Literature 1946.
Hermann Hesse published 'Siddhartha' in 1922, about the spiritual search of a young Indian man.
Günter Grass published 'The Tin Drum' in 1959. Nobel Prize in Literature 1999.
Charles Baudelaire published 'The Flowers of Evil' in 1857. It began modern poetry.
Arthur Rimbaud published 'A Season in Hell' in 1873, at just 19 years old.

question_en: How many cantos does Dante's 'Divine Comedy' have?
context_en: 'The Divine Comedy' has 100 cantos: 34 in the Inferno and 33 each in Purgatorio and Paradiso.
Fyodor Dostoevsky published 'The Brothers Karamazov' in 1880, his last novel.
Leo Tolstoy published 'Resurrection' in 1899.

question_en: Which Russian author wrote 'Fathers and Sons'?
context_en: Ivan Turgenev published 'Fathers and Sons' in 1862, a key novel of Russian realism.
